zones.label: Digital Signal
zones.summary: A square wave with only two states — LOW or HIGH
facts: Values: only two states — LOW (0 V) or HIGH (3.3 V)
Noise immunity: excellent — binary states ignore small fluctuations
Resolution: 1 bit per sample
Examples: LED on/off, button press, UART data bit, I2C clock
MicroPython read: pin.value() returns 0 or 1
MicroPython write: pin.value(1) or pin.value(0)
Pico GPIO: all 26 GPIO pins

zones.label: Analog Signal
zones.summary: A smooth wave that varies continuously from 0 V to 3.3 V
facts: Values: continuous range from 0 V to 3.3 V
Noise immunity: poor — susceptible to electromagnetic interference
Resolution: infinite in nature; limited by ADC when digitized
Examples: potentiometer wiper, microphone output, LDR voltage divider, temperature sensor
MicroPython read: ADC(pin).read_u16() returns 0–65535 (scaled from 12-bit hardware)
MicroPython write: not available (use PWM to approximate)
Pico GPIO: ADC only on GP26, GP27, GP28

zones.label: PWM — Digital Pretending to be Analog
zones.summary: Rapid HIGH/LOW pulses that slow devices perceive as analog
facts: Values: rapid switching between 0 V and 3.3 V; perceived as analog by slow devices
Duty cycle: 0% = always LOW; 50% = equal on/off; 100% = always HIGH
Frequency: 8 Hz to 62.5 MHz on RP2040
Resolution: 16-bit (0–65535 steps)
Examples: LED brightness (eye averages the flicker), servo position (1–2 ms pulse), motor speed
MicroPython: pwm = PWM(pin); pwm.duty_u16(32768) sets 50% duty
Pico GPIO: all 26 GPIO pins (8 PWM slices, 2 channels each)

zones.label: ADC & PWM Conversion Bridge
zones.summary: How the Pico crosses between the analog and digital worlds
facts: ADC converts Analog → Digital
PWM simulates Analog using Digital
ADC input is read with ADC(pin).read_u16() returning 0–65535
PWM output is set with pwm.duty_u16() between 0 and 65535
ADC reads real-world voltages into the program; PWM fakes voltages back out
RP2040 ADC is 12-bit hardware; PWM duty cycle is 16-bit

quiz.question: Which signal type has only two states, HIGH and LOW?
quiz.explanation: A digital signal is a square wave that is either LOW (0 V) or HIGH (3.3 V) — just 1 bit per sample. Because it only has two states, it ignores small voltage fluctuations and has excellent noise immunity.

quiz.question: Which signal type varies smoothly across a continuous range from 0 V to 3.3 V?
quiz.explanation: An analog signal changes continuously between 0 V and 3.3 V, like the voltage from a potentiometer or microphone. On the Pico you read it with an ADC pin (GP26, GP27, or GP28).

quiz.question: Which technique rapidly switches a pin between 0 V and 3.3 V so a slow device perceives it as analog?
quiz.explanation: PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) flips the pin HIGH and LOW very fast. The fraction of time it stays HIGH — the duty cycle — sets the perceived level, letting you dim an LED or position a servo with a 16-bit value (0–65535).

quiz.question: Where on the poster does it explain that ADC converts Analog to Digital while PWM simulates Analog using Digital?
quiz.explanation: The dark banner across the bottom is the conversion bridge: ADC turns real-world analog voltages into numbers (Analog → Digital), and PWM fakes analog output using fast digital pulses (Digital → Analog).
